request.question: While Michel Foucault  was a French philosopher, what was Kenneth Grahame?

title: The Order of Things
Kenneth Grahame
Security, Territory, Population
Discipline and Punish
The Archaeology of Knowledge
Joseph Tanke
The Hermeneutics of the Subject
Michel Foucault
The Passion of Michel Foucault
The Birth of Biopolitics
sentences: The Order of Things: An Archaeology of the Human Sciences (French: "Les mots et les choses: Une archéologie des sciences humaines" ) is a 1966 book by the French philosopher Michel Foucault.
It was translated into English and published by Pantheon Books in 1970.
(Foucault had preferred "L'Ordre des Choses" for the original French title, but changed the title because it had been used by two structuralist works published immediately prior to Foucault's).
Kenneth Grahame ( ; 8 March 1859 – 6 July 1932) was a Scottish writer, most famous for "The Wind in the Willows" (1908), one of the classics of children's literature.

He also wrote "The Reluctant Dragon"; both books were later adapted for stage and film, of which A.A. Milne's "Toad of Toad Hall" was the first, and the Disney films, which are "The Adventures of Ichabod and Mr. Toad" and "The Reluctant Dragon" are best known.

Security, Territory, Population is a part of a lecture series given by French philosopher Michel Foucault at the Collège de France between 1977 and 1978 and published posthumously based on audio recordings.

In it, Foucault examines the notion of biopolitics as a new technology of power over populations that is distinct from punitive disciplinary systems, by tracing the history of governmentality, from the first centuries of the Christian era to the emergence of the modern nation state.
These lectures illustrate a radical turning point in Foucault's work in which a shift to the problematic of the government of self and others occurred.
Discipline and Punish: The Birth of the Prison (French: "Surveiller et punir: Naissance de la prison" ) is a 1975 book by the French philosopher Michel Foucault.
It is an analysis of the social and theoretical mechanisms behind the changes that occurred in Western penal systems during the modern age based on historical documents from France.
Foucault argues that prison did not become the principal form of punishment just because of the humanitarian concerns of reformists.
He traces the cultural shifts that led to the predominance of prison via the body and power.
Prison used by the "disciplines" - new technological powers that can also be found, according to Foucault, in places such as schools, hospitals, and military barracks.

The Archaeology of Knowledge (French: "L'archéologie du savoir" ) is a 1969 methodological and historiographical treatise by the French philosopher Michel Foucault, in which he promotes "archaeology" or the "archaeological method", an analytical method he implicitly used in his previous works "Madness and Civilization" (1961), "The Birth of the Clinic" (1963), and "The Order of Things" (1966).
It is Foucault's only explicitly methodological work.
Joseph Tanke (Ph.D.
, Boston College; born 1978) is an American philosopher and Associate Professor of Philosophy at the University of Hawaii at Manoa.
His work focuses on Continental philosophy, the history of philosophy, aesthetics, art theory, and historical ontology.
Tanke is known for his analysis of major figures in the French philosophical tradition, such as Michel Foucault and Jacques Rancière.
His book Foucault's Philosophy of Art: A Genealogy of Modernity is one of the first systematic presentations of Michel Foucault's writings on visual art.
Tanke is the first scholar to publish a comprehensive critical introduction to the thought of Jacques Rancière.
The Hermeneutics of the Subject is a lecture course originally given by the French philosopher and historian Michel Foucault at the Collège de France in the years 1981-1982.
The course details Foucault's elaboration of such concepts as "practices of the self" and the "", as manifested in what Foucault refers to as their "golden age" in Hellenistic Greece and early Rome.
Foucault argues that this period put much more emphasis on taking "care" of oneself ("prendre soin de soi-même") than knowing oneself, whereas the converse is true today.
"The Hermeneutics of the Subject" has been influential in terms of understanding the late Foucault's "ethical turn", and has increasingly been attracting more general philosophical attention.
Paul-Michel Foucault (15 October 1926 – 25 June 1984), generally known as Michel Foucault (] ), was a French philosopher, historian of ideas, social theorist, and literary critic.
The Passion of Michel Foucault is a biography of the French philosopher Michel Foucault authored by the American philosopher James Miller.
It was first published in the United States by Simon & Schuster in 1993.
The Birth of Biopolitics is a part of a lecture series by French philosopher Michel Foucault at the Collège de France between 1978 and 1979 and published posthumously based on audio recordings.

In it, Foucault develops further the notion of biopolitics introduced in a previous lecture series, "Security, Territory, Population", by tracing the ways in which the eighteenth-century political economy marked the birth of a new governmental rationality.

Foucault uses the term Governmentality and raising questions of Political science, political philosophy and social policy concerning the role and status of the State and neo-liberalism in twentieth century politics.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: While Michel Foucault  was a French philosopher, what was Kenneth Grahame?
hop1_docs: The Passion of Michel Foucault | The Passion of Michel Foucault is a biography of the French philosopher Michel Foucault authored by the American philosopher James Miller.  It was first published in the United States by Simon & Schuster in 1993.
Michel Foucault | Paul-Michel Foucault (15 October 1926 – 25 June 1984), generally known as Michel Foucault (] ), was a French philosopher, historian of ideas, social theorist, and literary critic.

What Is an Author? | "What Is an Author?"  (French: "Qu’est-ce qu’un auteur?")  is a lecture on literary theory given at the Collège de France on 22 February 1969 by French philosopher, sociologist and historian Michel Foucault.

The Hermeneutics of the Subject | The Hermeneutics of the Subject is a lecture course originally given by the French philosopher and historian Michel Foucault at the Collège de France in the years 1981-1982.  The course details Foucault's elaboration of such concepts as "practices of the self" and the "", as manifested in what Foucault refers to as their "golden age" in Hellenistic Greece and early Rome.  Foucault argues that this period put much more emphasis on taking "care" of oneself ("prendre soin de soi-même") than knowing oneself, whereas the converse is true today.  "The Hermeneutics of the Subject" has been influential in terms of understanding the late Foucault's "ethical turn", and has increasingly been attracting more general philosophical attention.

Michel Foucault bibliography | Michel Foucault (1926–1984) was a prominent twentieth-century French philosopher, who wrote prolifically.  Many of his works were translated into English.  Works from his later years remain unpublished.

Joseph Tanke | Joseph Tanke (Ph.D. , Boston College; born 1978) is an American philosopher and Associate Professor of Philosophy at the University of Hawaii at Manoa.  His work focuses on Continental philosophy, the history of philosophy, aesthetics, art theory, and historical ontology.  Tanke is known for his analysis of major figures in the French philosophical tradition, such as Michel Foucault and Jacques Rancière.  His book Foucault's Philosophy of Art: A Genealogy of Modernity is one of the first systematic presentations of Michel Foucault's writings on visual art.  Tanke is the first scholar to publish a comprehensive critical introduction to the thought of Jacques Rancière.
Power-knowledge | Power-knowledge (French: "le savoir-pouvoir" ) is a concept coined by the French philosopher Michel Foucault.

- Power-knowledge — [7]: "is a concept coined by the French philosopher Michel Foucault"

Ambiguities:
- "Kenneth Grahame" — not mentioned in any passage; identity/status (occupation, nationality) is unresolved.
- No conflicting values for Michel Foucault in the passages; his roles are consistently given.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do not state Kenneth Grahame's occupation/profession or nationality (the relation required to answer "what was Kenneth Grahame?").

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the page "Kenneth Grahame" (Wikipedia article) 
- Search query: "Kenneth Grahame occupation" or "Kenneth Grahame biography"
- Search query: "Kenneth Grahame The Wind in the Willows author" 
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Kenny & the Dragon | Kenny & the Dragon is a 2008 children's novel by Tony DiTerlizzi.  It is based on the story of The Reluctant Dragon.  DiTerlizzi named the two protagonists Kenneth and Grahame, after the original story's author Kenneth Grahame.  He also includes references to Grahame's other famous work, "The Wind in the Willows".
Kenneth Graham (trade unionist) | Kenneth Graham (18 July 1922 – 15 July 2005) was a British trade union leader.
Kenneth Graham Jupp | Sir Kenneth Graham Jupp MC (2 June 1917 – 15 March 2004), was a British Judge and a Liberal Party politician.
Kenneth Bevan | Kenneth Graham Bevan was an Anglican missionary bishop.

The Adventures of Ichabod and Mr. Toad | The Adventures of Ichabod and Mr. Toad is a 1949 animated package film produced by Walt Disney Productions and released by RKO Radio Pictures.  The film consists of two segments – the first is based on the 1908 children's novel "The Wind in the Willows" by Scottish author Kenneth Grahame, and the second is based on the 1820 short story "The Legend of Sleepy Hollow," called "Ichabod Crane" in the film, by American author Washington Irving.

Kenneth Grahame | Kenneth Grahame ( ; 8 March 1859 – 6 July 1932) was a Scottish writer, most famous for "The Wind in the Willows" (1908), one of the classics of children's literature.  He also wrote "The Reluctant Dragon"; both books were later adapted for stage and film, of which A.A. Milne's "Toad of Toad Hall" was the first, and the Disney films, which are "The Adventures of Ichabod and Mr. Toad" and "The Reluctant Dragon" are best known.

Kenneth G. Ross | Kenneth Graham Ross (born 4 June 1941) is an Australian playwright, screenwriter, and lyricist best known for writing the 1978 stage play "Breaker Morant", that was based on the life of Australian soldier Harry "Breaker" Morant.
